3.1 Study Site

Dixon Glacier (59.66°N, 150.88°W) is a large valley glacier on the Kenai Peninsula, south-central Alaska, with an area of approximately 40.1 km² (RGI7, ID RGI2000-v7.0-G-01-07538). The glacier spans 439 to 1637 m elevation and has been losing mass throughout the satellite era.

Mass balance observations have been collected at three stake sites since 2023, spanning the ablation zone to the accumulation zone. An on-glacier automatic weather station provides summer temperature data at the ELA site for lapse rate validation.

Stake and Station Locations
SiteTypeElevationNotes
ABLAblation stake804 mMain glacier trunk, strong summer ablation
ELAELA stake1078 mSouthern branch; wind-loaded accumulation zone
ACCAccumulation stake1293 mUpper glacier, net annual accumulation
Dixon AWSWeather station1078 mAt ELA site; summer only (May–Sep)
Nuka SNOTELClimate forcing375 mSite 1037, ~10 km NW; daily T & P since 1990
Elevation Corrections (D-013, D-023)
Two critical elevation errors were discovered and corrected during model development: Nuka SNOTEL was recorded as 1230 m but is actually 375 m (NRCS reports in feet: 1230 ft = 375 m). The Dixon AWS was recorded at 804 m (ABL) but is actually at 1078 m (ELA site). These corrections resolved all early calibration failures (CAL-001 through CAL-007).
Digitized Glacier Outlines (2000–2025)

Six glacier outlines were manually digitized from historical satellite imagery at 5-year intervals, providing area evolution checkpoints for the behavioral filter:

YearArea (km²)Change from 2000
200040.11—
200540.110.00
201039.83−0.28
201539.26−0.85
202038.59−1.52
202538.34−1.77

Total retreat 2000–2025: 1.77 km² (4.4%)

3.2 Model Description

We apply a Distributed Enhanced Temperature Index Model (DETIM) following Method 2 of Hock (1999)D-001: Model Selection
Balances physical realism (radiation + temperature) against data availability. Dixon Glacier lacks the full energy balance data needed for DEBAM. Method 2 adds spatially distributed potential clear-sky radiation to a basic degree-day model, capturing topographic shading and aspect effects. Alternatives: classical degree-day (too simple for 40 km² with 1200m elevation range) and full energy balance (requires wind, humidity, albedo, cloud cover at grid scale).. Daily melt M (mm w.e. d−1) at each grid cell is computed using a temperature index enhanced by potential clear-sky direct solar radiation.

3.2.1 Melt Equation

M = (MF + rsnow/ice × Ipot) × T    when T > 0°C
M = 0                                         when T ≤ 0°C
MF = melt factor (mm d−1 K−1), varies with elevation: MF(z) = MF + MFgrad × (z − zref)
rsnow/ice = radiation factor for snow or ice (mm m² W−1 d−1 K−1), with rice = 2.0 × rsnow
Ipot = potential clear-sky direct solar radiation (W m−2)
T = distributed air temperature (°C)
Elevation-Dependent Melt Factor Why?

A single MF cannot capture the ABL-to-ACC mass balance gradient. Integrated effects of albedo, wind, humidity, and cloud cover cause melt efficiency to decrease with elevation. MFgrad adds one parameter to capture this (D-008D-008: Elevation-Dependent Melt Factor
MF(z) = MF + MF_grad * (z - z_ref). Negative MF_grad = less melt at higher elevations. Bounds: [-0.01, 0.0] mm d&sup-1; K&sup-1; per m. Floor at 0.1 mm d&sup-1; K&sup-1;.).

MAP value: MFgrad = −0.00411 mm d−1 K−1 m−1

3.2.2 Temperature Distribution

Tcell = Tstation + λ × (zcell − zstation)
λ = lapse rate, fixed at −5.0 °C km−1 (Gardner & Sharp 2009, Roth et al. 2023)
zstation = Nuka SNOTEL elevation = 375 m
zcell = DEM elevation of grid cell
Why is the lapse rate fixed? Why?
Equifinality (D-015, D-017)
Preliminary calibration (CAL-001 through CAL-009) revealed persistent equifinality between the lapse rate and precipitation correction factor: a steep lapse rate with low precipitation correction produces similar current-climate mass balance as a moderate lapse rate with higher precipitation. However, these parameter sets diverge under warming — the steep-lapse solution underestimates warming at high elevations.

CAL-009 converged to λ = −6.83 °C/km with precip_corr = 1.20 — compensating errors that fit current observations but would produce unreliable projections. Literature convergence on −5.0 °C/km for maritime Alaskan glaciers provides a stronger constraint than the calibration data.

3.2.3 Precipitation Distribution

Pcell = Pstation × Cp × (1 + γp × Δz)
Cp = precipitation correction factor (gauge undercatch + spatial transfer), MAP = 1.62
γp = precipitation gradient (fraction m−1), MAP = 0.000694
Δz = zcell − zstation

3.2.4 Rain/Snow Partitioning

fsnow = 1.0                          if T < T0 − 1°C
fsnow = 0.5 × (T0 + 1 − T)   if T0 − 1 ≤ T ≤ T0 + 1°C
fsnow = 0.0                          if T > T0 + 1°C
T0 = rain/snow threshold temperature, MAP = ~0.0 °C (at reference elevation)
Linear transition over a 2°C window centered on T0

3.2.5 Solar Radiation

Potential clear-sky direct radiation Ipot is computed for each grid cell and day of year following Oke (1987) solar geometry with topographic corrections for slope, aspect, and self-shading (Hock, 1999). Atmospheric transmissivity is set to ψa = 0.75. Radiation values are precomputed for all 365 days at 3-hour intervals and stored in a lookup table.

3.2.6 Snowpack and Surface Type

A snow water equivalent (SWE) layer is tracked at each grid cell. Surface type determines which radiation factor is applied:

  • Snow (SWE > 0): rsnow
  • Firn (SWE = 0, elevation ≥ median glacier elevation): rsnow
  • Ice (SWE = 0, below firn line): rice = 2.0 × rsnow
Glacier Dynamics — Delta-h Parameterization D-018

Glacier area and volume evolution are tracked using the delta-h parameterization of Huss et al. (2010). Ice thickness is initialized from Farinotti et al. (2019) consensus estimates. Cells deglaciate when ice thickness falls below 1 m.

Δh = (hr + a)γ + b × (hr + a) + c
hr = normalized elevation: (zmax − z) / (zmax − zmin)
Large glacier (>20 km²): γ=6, a=−0.02, b=0.12, c=0.00
Dynamic size class switching as glacier shrinks past thresholds (20, 5 km²)

Volume-area scaling: V = 0.0340 × A1.36 (Bahr et al. 1997)

3.3 Input Data

3.3.1 Digital Elevation Model

IfSAR 2010, 5 m resolution, resampled to 100 m model grid (289 × 117 cells, 4,011 glacier cells). Source: USGS 3DEP program.

3.3.2 Climate Forcing

The primary climate station is the Nuka Glacier SNOTEL (site 1037, 375 m), located approximately 10 km northwest of Dixon Glacier. Daily mean air temperature and precipitation are available from October 1990 to present, with significant gaps in early years.

Multi-Station Gap-Fill Cascade (D-025)
Critical Fix: Gap-Fill Pipeline
The original approach (ffill().fillna(0)) set summer temperatures to 0°C during gaps, killing melt and poisoning calibration. For WY2005, 86% of melt-season temperature was missing — the model produced zero melt for the entire summer.

Solution: cascade gap-fill using 5 nearby SNOTEL stations with monthly reverse regressions (Tnuka = slope × Tother + intercept):

StationElevationDistanceT coverageRole
Middle Fork Bradley701 m16 km6.0%Primary fill (best T predictor, r=0.877)
McNeil Canyon411 m24 km1.8%Secondary fill, covers WY2001 gaps
Anchor River Divide503 m34 km0.3%Tertiary fill
Kachemak Creek503 m14 km—Discontinued 2019
Lower Kachemak Ck597 m13 km—Since 2015 only

Final record: 9,862 days (WY1999–WY2025), zero missing values. Nuka provides 91.3% of temperature days and 98.0% of precipitation days.

3.3.3 Stake Observations

SiteYearAnnual (m w.e.)SummerWinterσNotes
ABL (804m)WY2023−4.50−5.35+0.85±0.12Measured
ABL (804m)WY2024−2.63−4.56+1.93±0.12Measured
ELA (1078m)WY2023+0.10−2.26+2.36±0.12Measured
ELA (1078m)WY2024+0.10−2.50+2.60±0.12Measured
ACC (1293m)WY2023+0.37−2.25+2.45±0.12Measured
ACC (1293m)WY2024+1.46−1.55+3.01±0.12Measured
ACC (1293m)WY2025+1.88−1.66+3.53±0.15Estimated
ELA (1078m)WY2025+1.08−1.96+3.04±0.12Estimated

3.3.4 Geodetic Mass Balance

PerioddM/dt (m w.e. yr−1)UncertaintyUsage
2000–2020−0.939±0.122Calibration target
2000–2010−1.072±0.225Validation only
2010–2020−0.806±0.202Validation only

Source: Hugonnet et al. (2021), RGI60-01.18059. Sub-periods are NOT statistically distinguishable (Z = 0.88, p > 0.30).

Why only use 2000–2020? D-016

Using all 3 periods is statistically improper — the 20-year value is derived from the sub-periods and is not independent. Additionally, the sub-periods create contradictory constraints: Nuka SNOTEL shows cooler summers in 2001–2010 (9.07°C mean) than 2011–2020 (10.00°C), so the model produces less melt in the first decade. But Hugonnet shows MORE mass loss in 2001–2010. This contradiction is within reported uncertainty.

3.3.5 Snowline Observations

22 years of digitized snowline shapefiles (1999–2024), extracted from end-of-summer satellite imagery. Observed spatial standard deviation: 24–69 m within a year; interannual standard deviation: 63 m.

3.4 Calibration

Model calibration employs a two-phase Bayesian approach following Rounce et al. (2020): differential evolution (DE) identifies the maximum a posteriori (MAP) parameter estimate, then Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling maps the posterior distribution to generate an ensemble of parameter sets for projection uncertainty quantification.

CAL-013: Multi-Objective Calibration (D-028)
Phase 1: 5 DE seeds × 200 iterations → 1 mode identified (all seeds converge)
Phase 2: 24 MCMC walkers × 10,000 steps, burn-in 2,000, thin by autocorrelation time (117)
Phase 3: Post-hoc area filter (1,000 candidates → all 1,000 pass at RMSE ≤ 1.0 km²)
Result: 1,656 posterior samples, acceptance fraction 0.368
Total wall time: ~20 hours (DE ~8.8 hr, MCMC ~11.2 hr)

3.4.1 Calibrated and Fixed Parameters

Calibrated parameters (6):

ParameterSymbolUnitsBoundsPriorMAPPosterior [5%, 95%]
Melt factorMFmm d−1 K−1[1, 12]N(5.0, 3.0)7.117.30 [7.06, 7.58]
MF gradientMFgradmm d−1 K−1 m−1[−0.01, 0]Uniform−0.00411—
Radiation factor (snow)rsnowmm m² W−1 d−1 K−1[0.02, 1.5]×10−3Uniform0.00196—
Precip gradientγpm−1[0.0002, 0.006]Uniform0.000694—
Precip correctionCp—[1.2, 4.0]Uniform1.621.61 [1.47, 1.74]
Rain/snow thresholdT0°C[0.5, 3.0]N(1.5, 0.5)~0.0—

Fixed parameters (2):

ParameterValueSourceRationale
Lapse rate λ−5.0 °C km−1Gardner & Sharp (2009), Roth et al. (2023)Prevents equifinality with Cp
rice/rsnow ratio2.0Hock (1999) Table 4Preserves albedo feedback for projections

3.4.2 Calibration Targets

  • Stake annual balance: 8 observations at 3 elevations (WY2023–2025)
  • Stake summer balance: 8 observations
  • Stake winter balance: 9 observations
  • Geodetic mass balance: Hugonnet et al. (2021) 2000–2020: −0.939 ± 0.122 m w.e. yr−1
  • Snowline elevation: 22 years of digitized snowlines (σ = 75 m)

3.4.3 Likelihood Function

ln L(θ) = −0.5 × Σi [(mi(θ) − oi) / σi]²
mi(θ) = modeled value for parameter vector θ
oi = observed value
σi = reported measurement uncertainty
Inverse-variance weighting naturally gives more weight to precise observations.
Geodetic hard penalty (λ = 50) applied when residual exceeds reported uncertainty.
Figure 1. Parameter posterior distributions from CAL-013 (1,656 samples). Corner plot shows marginal distributions and pairwise correlations for the 6 calibrated parameters. Red lines indicate MAP estimates. Notable correlation between MF and precip_corr reflects the expected trade-off between melt intensity and accumulation.
Figure 2. Observed vs. modeled stake mass balance for MAP parameters. Annual, summer, and winter balances at three elevations (ABL 804m, ELA 1078m, ACC 1293m). Note the persistent negative bias at ELA — see Section 3.5 and D-031 for discussion.
Equifinality Discussion (D-017)

Preliminary calibration (CAL-001 through CAL-009) revealed persistent equifinality between the lapse rate and precipitation correction factor. Two key trade-offs were identified:

  1. Lapse rate vs. precipitation: Steep lapse + low precip_corr ≈ moderate lapse + high precip_corr for current climate, but these diverge under warming.
  2. MF vs. r_snow: High MF + low r_snow ≈ low MF + high r_snow for current radiation geometry, but differ as the snowline retreats and more ice is exposed.

Solution: fix λ at −5.0 °C/km and derive rice from rsnow, eliminating the two primary equifinality axes while maintaining 6 free parameters.

3.5 Validation

3.5.1 Sub-Period Geodetic Comparison

The Hugonnet sub-periods serve as independent validation (only 2000–2020 was used for calibration):

PeriodTypeObserved (m w.e./yr)Modeled (median)BiasWithin unc.?
2000–2020Calibration−0.939 ± 0.122−0.765+0.174No (1.4σ)
2000–2010Validation−1.072 ± 0.225−0.244+0.828No
2010–2020Validation−0.806 ± 0.202−1.287−0.481No
Interpretation of Sub-Period Mismatch

The model reverses the sub-period trend: it underestimates mass loss 2000–2010 and overestimates 2010–2020. This is consistent with D-016: Nuka SNOTEL shows cooler summers in 2001–2010 (9.07°C) vs 2011–2020 (10.00°C), so the model produces less melt in the first decade. But Hugonnet shows MORE mass loss in 2000–2010 than 2010–2020.

The contradiction likely reflects gap-filled climate quality in early years (WY2001: 77% T missing, WY2005: 43%), where fill stations may underestimate precipitation events at the glacier.

Figure 3. Geodetic sub-period validation. Observed (Hugonnet et al. 2021) vs. modeled glacier-wide mass balance for calibration period (2000–2020) and validation sub-periods (2000–2010, 2010–2020). Error bars show reported uncertainty.

3.5.2 Posterior Predictive Check by Year

YearSiteObserved (m w.e.)Modeled (median)ResidualStatus
WY2023ABL−4.50−4.12+0.38Good
WY2023ACC+0.37+0.42+0.05Excellent
WY2023ELA+0.10−1.31−1.41Biased (wind)
WY2024ABL−2.63−4.24−1.61Forcing issue
WY2024ACC+1.46+0.21−1.25Forcing issue
WY2024ELA+0.10−1.42−1.52Biased (wind + forcing)
WY2025ACC+1.88+2.62+0.74Estimated obs
WY2025ELA+1.08+0.18−0.90Estimated obs

Overall RMSE (measured only, n=6): 1.20 m w.e.

3.5.3 ELA Wind Redistribution Bias (D-031)

Known Spatial Bias: −1.4 m w.e. at ELA

The ELA stake (1078 m) exhibits a persistent negative bias of approximately −1.4 m w.e. in both WY2023 and WY2024, while ABL and ACC residuals are small. This is attributed to wind redistribution: digitized snowlines show systematically greater accumulation on the southern branch where the ELA stake is located, consistent with preferential wind-loading from ESE storms.

The model distributes precipitation uniformly by elevation and predicts the average balance across all 814 cells at 1028–1128 m (20.3% of glacier area). The stake observation represents a single point in a locally favored accumulation zone.

The glacier-wide geodetic constraint (within 2σ of Hugonnet) confirms the integrated mass balance is well-captured. This is a measurement representativity limitation, not a calibration failure.

WY2024 Forcing Limitation (separate issue)

WY2024 shows large residuals at ALL sites (not just ELA). Root cause: Nuka SNOTEL recorded 912 mm winter precipitation (similar to WY2023's 864 mm), but observed winter balance at Dixon was dramatically higher (ABL: 0.85 → 1.93 m w.e., +127%). The off-glacier forcing station missed a local accumulation event.

The precipitation gradient also flattened from 38%/100m (WY2023) to 11%/100m (WY2024), confirming the interannual variability that a single precip_grad cannot capture.

3.5.4 Sensitivity of Fixed Parameters

Figure 4. Sensitivity of geodetic mass balance and stake RMSE to perturbation of fixed parameters. Left: lapse rate (−4.0 to −6.5 °C/km). Right: rice/rsnow ratio (1.5–3.0). Lapse rate sensitivity is ~10× larger than the ratio.
Full Sensitivity Table

Lapse rate sensitivity:

λ (°C/km)Geodetic modeledBiasStake RMSE
−4.0−1.631−0.6921.800
−4.5−1.216−0.2771.497
−5.0 (used)−0.817+0.1221.227
−5.5−0.434+0.5051.005
−6.0−0.063+0.8760.850
−6.5+0.296+1.2350.781

rice/rsnow ratio sensitivity:

RatioGeodetic modeledBiasStake RMSE
1.50−0.773+0.1661.188
2.00 (used)−0.817+0.1221.227
2.50−0.862+0.0771.271
3.00−0.906+0.0331.318

Key finding: Lapse rate sensitivity is ~10× larger than rice/rsnow. Geodetic bias swings 1.9 m w.e./yr across the lapse range vs 0.13 for the ratio. The −5.0 °C/km choice sits near the minimum geodetic bias.

3.6 Historical Reconstruction

The model is run for WY1999–WY2025 using the gap-filled climate forcing and 250 posterior parameter sets to reconstruct historical mass balance and trends.

−0.80
Mean annual MB (m w.e./yr)
−0.92/decade
Annual trend (p=0.007)
−0.49/decade
Summer trend (p=0.023)
Figure 5. Historical mass balance reconstruction (WY1999–WY2025). Top: winter (blue), summer (red), and annual (black) mass balance. Bottom: cumulative mass balance with posterior uncertainty envelope. The geodetic constraint (−0.939 ± 0.122 m w.e./yr, 2000–2020) is shown for reference.
Figure 6. Mass balance trends with significance tests. Annual balance trend: −0.92 m w.e. per decade (p = 0.007, statistically significant). Summer balance trend: −0.49 per decade (p = 0.023). Winter balance shows no significant trend, indicating mass loss is driven by increasing summer ablation rather than decreasing accumulation.

3.7 Projections

3.7.1 Ensemble Design

Projections propagate both parameter uncertainty and climate uncertainty:

  • 250 parameter sets from the MCMC posterior (top-performing by log-probability)
  • 5 GCMs: ACCESS-CM2, EC-Earth3, MPI-ESM1-2-HR, MRI-ESM2-0, NorESM2-MM
  • 3 SSPs: SSP1-2.6, SSP2-4.5, SSP5-8.5
  • Total: 250 × 5 = 1,250 runs per SSP (3,750 total)

Climate forcing: NASA NEX-GDDP-CMIP6 (0.25°, daily, bias-corrected), with monthly delta method calibration against Nuka SNOTEL 1991–2020 climatology.

Figure 7. Projected glacier area evolution under three SSP scenarios (2025–2100). Shaded bands show 5th–95th percentile credible intervals spanning both parameter and GCM uncertainty. Circles indicate historical digitized outlines for context.

3.7.2 End-of-Century Results

ScenarioLapse RateArea 2100 (km²) [p50]Range [p05–p95]% RemainingPeak Water Year
SSP2-4.5−4.518.215.8–23.945%2058
SSP2-4.5−5.022.018.9–29.355%2058
SSP2-4.5−5.527.222.5–33.668%2061
SSP5-8.5−4.58.66.2–21.521%2061
SSP5-8.5−5.012.111.3–25.930%2063
SSP5-8.5−5.514.914.1–31.237%2065
Peak Water Timing
Peak water (maximum glacier-fed discharge) occurs between WY2058 and WY2065 depending on scenario and lapse rate. Under SSP5-8.5, peak discharge is approximately 9.1 m³/s (11-year running mean), after which glacier runoff declines permanently as ice volume diminishes.

3.7.3 Lapse Rate Sensitivity Bracket

To bracket structural uncertainty from the fixed lapse rate, projections are run at −4.5, −5.0, and −5.5 °C/km using the same v13 posterior parameters (D-030D-030: Lapse Rate Sensitivity Projections
Rather than recalibrating (which re-introduces equifinality), we run scenario-based sensitivity projections: the same v13 posterior params at each lapse rate. This shows readers the projection envelope attributable to lapse rate uncertainty. Total: 3 × 2 × 250 × 5 = 7,500 simulations.).

Figure 8. Lapse rate sensitivity bracket for glacier area projections. Three lapse rates (−4.5, −5.0, −5.5 °C/km) spanning the literature range for maritime Alaskan glaciers. The lapse rate introduces a ~9 km² spread in SSP5-8.5 end-of-century area, comparable to the spread between SSP scenarios.

3.8 Implementation

The model is implemented in Python 3.12 using NumPy for array operations and Numba for just-in-time compilation of the simulation kernel.

~240 ms
Per water-year simulation
4,011
Glacier grid cells
289 × 117
Grid dimensions
100 m
Grid resolution
Code Structure
ModulePurpose
dixon_melt/config.pySite-specific configuration and physical constants
dixon_melt/fast_model.pyNumba JIT-compiled simulation kernel (run_simulation)
dixon_melt/solar.pyClear-sky radiation lookup table (Oke 1987)
dixon_melt/terrain.pyDEM processing, slope/aspect, wind exposure (Sx)
dixon_melt/climate.pyClimate data loading and preprocessing
dixon_melt/glacier_dynamics.pyDelta-h parameterization (Huss et al. 2010)
dixon_melt/climate_projections.pyCMIP6 bias correction and ensemble loading
dixon_melt/calibration.pyLikelihood, priors, objective function
dixon_melt/routing.pyParallel linear reservoir discharge model
run_calibration_v13.pyCurrent calibration script (DE+MCMC+area filter)
run_projection.pyEnsemble projection pipeline
run_validation.pyValidation suite (geodetic, stakes, sensitivity)
